steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: 'lobbying is not as ______ as this finding seems to suggest.' The finding is that lobbying does little to change the probability of policies being enacted. The blank must name what that finding implies about lobbying — and the sentence then pushes back by noting lobbying still shapes which proposals come forward.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'counterproductive': means actively causing harm or making things worse. The finding shows lobbying has little effect, not that it backfires — this overstates the criticism. ✗
  • Choice B — 'cynical': means distrustful or believing people act from selfish motives. The finding is about lobbying's effectiveness on enactment rates, not anyone's intentions. ✗
  • Choice C — 'biased': means favoring one side unfairly. The finding concerns statistical probability, not fairness or objectivity. ✗
  • Choice D — 'ineffectual': means failing to produce the desired result. The finding — that lobbying barely moves enactment probability — implies lobbying is ineffectual. Kang's counterpoint (lobbying shapes which proposals reach the agenda) shows it is not as ineffectual as that finding alone suggests. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
